qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Unbounded resource allocation in XML parser enables denial-of-service via memory exhaustion
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding targets unbounded XML parsing and allocation in lib/sax.js, while the advisory targets unbounded recursive DOM traversals in lib/dom.js after parsing succeeds.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory root cause: unbounded pure-JavaScript recursive traversal in xmldom lib/dom.js methods such as XMLSerializer.serializeToString(), normalize(), cloneNode(true), importNode(), textContent, isEqualNode(), and getElementsBy* helpers. The manifest-scoped qualifying DoS report instead attributes impact to lib/sax.js parser resource allocation.
evidence: Advisory GHSA-2v35-w6hq-6mfw states that parsing deeply nested XML succeeds and denial of service occurs during later recursive DOM operations in lib/dom.js.

dos_memory_exhaustion/vulnerability_report.json lists code locations in lib/sax.js and describes DOMParser.parseFromString() parser allocation, parseStack growth, attribute flooding, and comment handling.

dos_memory_exhaustion/reproduction/exploit.js only parses generated XML with DOMParser.parseFromString() and does not call XMLSerializer.serializeToString() or any other advisory-listed recursive DOM traversal.
